content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
SENATE
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

DAVID ROBERTI
PRESIDENT PRO TEMPORE

May 16, 1991


Mr. Scott Hallabrin, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

Upon learning of your recent advice letter to Luke Fry
(F.P.P.C. File No. A-91-152) regarding a legal challenge
to an existing law created by the initiative process, and
after considering that advice as well as footnote #3 of
the Buchanan opinion (F.P.P.C. No. 78-013), I find that
I have several related questions.

The purpose of this letter is to seek advice as to whether or
not a contribution, as defined in the Political Reform Act of
1974, to me or one of my controlled committees occurs as a
consequence of any of the following actions. *

1. I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
with members of an organization which is one of the
parties named in the litigation.  That organization
and/or individual members thereof subsequently make
payments directly to the law firm representing my
position in the case.


*  For your information, I control several committees
including my Senate re-election committee, a general purpose
ballot measure committee, and a ballot measure committee
formed primarily to oppose Propositions 131 and 140.
Furthermore, I am a plaintiff in pending litigation
concerning both Propositions 73 and 140.  The Senate Rules
Committee, which I chair, voted to be a plaintiff in
litigation concerning Proposition 140.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Page 2
May 16, 1991


2.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
     with members of an organization which is not a party
     named in the litigation but which stands to benefit
     from it.  That organization and/or members thereof
     subsequently make payments directly to the law firm
     representing my position in the case.

3.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
     with members of an organization which is not a party
     named in the litigation but which stands to benefit
     from it.  That organization and/or members thereof
     subsequently convince other persons to make payments
     directly to the law firm representing my position in
     the case.

4.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
     with other officeholders who are named as parties in
     the litigation.  Those officeholders subsequently make
     payments directly to the law firm representing my
     position in the case.

5.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
     with other officeholders who are not parties named in the
     litigation but who stand to benefit from the litigation.
     Those officeholders subsequently make payments directly
     to the law firm representing my position in the case.

6.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 73
     with other officeholders who are not parties named in the
     litigation but who stand to benefit from the litigation.
     Those officeholders subsequently convince other persons
     to make payments directly to the law firm representing my
     position in the case.

7.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 140
     with members of an organization which is one of the
     parties named in the litigation.  That organization
     and/or individual members thereof subsequently make
     payments directly to the law firm representing my
     position in the case.

8.  I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 140
     with members of an organization which is not a party
     named in the litigation but which stands to benefit from
     it.  That organization and/or members thereof
     subsequently make payments directly to the law firm
     representing my position in the case.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Page 3
May 16, 1991


9. I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 140
with members of an organization which is not a party
named in the litigation but which stands to benefit from
it. That organization and/or members thereof
subsequently convince other persons to make payments
directly to the law firm representing my position in the
case.

10. I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 140
with other officeholders who are named as parties in the
litigation. Those officeholders subsequently make
payments directly to the law firm representing my
position in the case.

11. I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 140
with other officeholders who are not parties named in the
litigation but who stand to benefit from the litigation.
Those officeholders subsequently make payments directly
to the law firm representing my position in the case.

12. I discuss pending litigation concerning Proposition 140
with other officeholders who are not parties named in the
litigation but who stand to benefit from the litigation.
Those officeholders subsequently convince other persons
to make payments directly to the law firm representing my
position in the case.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this request. I look
forward to your reply.

Sincerely,

DAVID ROBERTI
President pro Tempore

DR:dcscjg

sections.question: Whether a contribution, as defined in the Political Reform Act of 1974, to me or one of my controlled committees occurs as a consequence of any of the following actions: [12 detailed scenarios involving discussions of pending litigation concerning Propositions 73 and 140 with various organizations, their members, and other officeholders, followed by those parties making or convincing others to make payments directly to the law firm representing the requestor's position in the cases].
sections.question_synthetic: Senator David Roberti seeks FPPC advice on whether various scenarios involving discussions of pending litigation regarding Propositions 73 and 140 with organizations, their members, and other officeholders—followed by those parties making or soliciting payments to his litigation counsel—would constitute contributions under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response letter. The document contains the complete QUESTION section (presented as 12 numbered scenarios) but no CONCLUSION section. The letter references a prior FPPC advice letter (A-91-152) to Luke Fry. Document shows OCR artifacts but content is clearly legible.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether a contribution, as defined in the Political Reform Act of 1974, to me or one of my controlled committees occurs as a consequence of any of the following actions: [12 detailed scenarios involving discussions of pending litigation concerning Propositions 73 and 140 with various organizations, their members, and other officeholders, followed by those parties making or convincing others to make payments directly to the law firm representing the requestor's position in the cases].

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from California State Senator David Roberti to the FPPC General Counsel seeking advice on whether certain discussions about pending litigation and subsequent payments to his legal representation would constitute contributions under the Political Reform Act. The request outlines 12 specific scenarios involving different parties and payment arrangements related to litigation over Propositions 73 and 140.
